Tissue Detail: Human adipose-derived MSCs (bone marrow protocol; clinically grade)
Methodology Detail: Post-thaw viability (7-AAD), cell count, light microscopy, flow cytometry
Title: Reconstitution and post-thaw storage of cryopreserved human MSCs: pitfalls and optimisations for clinically compatible formulants
Cryoprotectant / Method: 10% DMSO (standard); saline ± 2% HSA as reconstitution solutions
Post-Thaw Viability (%): >90% (7-AAD negative) in saline-HSA formulations
Cell Recovery (%): Cell loss significant when reconstituted in protein-free solution; stable ≥4 h in saline-HSA
Apoptosis Data: Not quantified separately
Surface Markers: CD73+, CD90+, CD105+ maintained; HI-MSC >98% 7-AAD+ at 24 h
Functional Assays: Cell stability over time (0–4 h), cell concentration effects (1×10⁴–5×10⁶/mL)
Gene Expression: Not reported
Key Quantitative Data Points: Cell loss at concentrations <5×10⁴/mL; no aggregation at wet ice vs. RT aggregation in culture medium; saline-HSA = culture medium in viability (p>0.05); MSC stability ≥4 h confirmed
Key Finding: Saline + 2% HSA is a clinically compatible, simple reconstitution solution. Protein-free solutions cause significant cell loss. RT storage causes aggregation; wet ice preferred.
Limitations: Adipose tissue source (not bone marrow); single centre; no functional potency testing
Clinical Relevance: High – directly applicable to GMP MSC reconstitution protocols

Tissue Detail: Human bone marrow-derived MSCs (BM-MSCs), commercial (AllCells)
Methodology Detail: Flow cytometry, Annexin V/PI apoptosis, CFU assay, T-cell proliferation, gene expression (qPCR), metabolic activity (MTS)
Title: Cryopreserved mesenchymal stem cells regain functional potency following a 24-h acclimation period
Cryoprotectant / Method: 90% FBS + 10% DMSO
Post-Thaw Viability (%): FT: ~80% (trypan blue); reduced vs FC (p<0.05). TT (24 h): comparable to FC
Cell Recovery (%): Not separately quantified
Apoptosis Data: FT: significantly more apoptotic (p<0.05) and necrotic (p<0.01) than FC/TT. TT: apoptosis significantly reduced vs FT (p<0.05)
Surface Markers: FT: CD44↓ and CD105↓ (p<0.05). FC and TT: no change in CD44, CD105, CD73, CD90
Functional Assays: MTS metabolic activity (FT↑ vs FC p<0.05); CFU-F (FT↓ vs FC p<0.05); T-cell proliferation inhibition (TT > FT, both vs. no-MSC control p<0.001)
Immunomodulation: All groups arrested T-cell proliferation; TT significantly more potent than FT (p<0.05); IFN-γ secretion: FT↓ vs FC (p<0.05)
Gene Expression: FT: ↓VEGF, ↓HGF, ↓SDF-1, ↓IL-10; TT: ↑VEGF, ↑HGF, ↑SDF-1 (reversal of FT suppression)
Key Quantitative Data Points: T-cell proliferation inhibition: FC ~60%, FT ~45%, TT ~65% (at 1:3 MSC:PBMC). IFN-γ: FC ~600 pg/mL, FT ~200 pg/mL, TT ~600 pg/mL
Statistical Tests: One-way ANOVA with Tukey post-hoc; p<0.05 threshold
Key Finding: Freshly thawed MSCs have reduced immunopotency and elevated apoptosis. 24-h acclimation fully restores CD surface markers, gene expression, and T-cell suppression.
Limitations: Single commercial BM donor source; 90% FBS (not clinically translatable); no in vivo data
Clinical Relevance: High – supports 24-h recovery period before clinical infusion

Tissue Detail: Human BM-MSCs (Emory University, multiple donors)
Methodology Detail: Flow cytometry (Ki67, 7-AAD, Annexin V, CFSE), ELISA (IDO), bioluminescence imaging (in vivo), single-cell cytotoxicity (PanToxiLux)
Title: Cryopreserved Mesenchymal Stromal Cells Are Susceptible to T-Cell Mediated Apoptosis Which Is Partly Rescued by IFNγ Licensing
Cryoprotectant / Method: 10% DMSO ± HSA; DMSO-free (HypoThermosol-based) tested as alternative
Freezing Rate: Step-down controlled-rate freezer (programme detailed in paper)
Post-Thaw Viability (%): Trypan blue viability comparable live vs cryo (~80–90%); 7-AAD viability similar
Cell Recovery (%): Total viable cell yield: similar between live and cryo groups (not significantly different)
Apoptosis Data: Cryo-MSCs: significant apoptosis/lysis upon co-culture with autologous PBMCs (p<0.05). Enhanced lysis with allogeneic PBMCs. Caspase inhibitor (Z-VAD-FMK) did not rescue.
Surface Markers: PDL1 expression confirmed post IFNγ pre-licensing
Functional Assays: T-cell proliferation (Ki67 staining): Cryo-MSCs attenuated suppression in direct co-culture vs transwell (p<0.05). IFNγ-licensed cryo-MSCs restored suppression to live-MSC level.
Immunomodulation: IDO activity: Live MSCs induced IDO; freshly thawed cryo-MSCs refractory to IFNγ-induced IDO. IFNγ pre-licensing rescues IDO induction (IDO inhibitor 1-MT blocks rescue, p<0.05). Cytotoxic T-cell degranulation: Cryo-MSCs failed to inhibit; IFNγ-licensed cryo-MSCs inhibited.
Gene Expression: HSP70A and HSP70B mRNA: significantly upregulated in cryo-MSCs vs live (p<0.05)
Key Quantitative Data Points: Lung tropism (bioluminescence): Live MSCs 100% reference; Cryo-MSCs 40±24%; IFNγ-Cryo 44±32% (not significantly different from Cryo). T-cell proliferation inhibition restored to ~live level with IFNγ pre-licensing.
Statistical Tests: One-way ANOVA, Tukey multiple comparison; two-tail t-tests; p<0.05
Key Finding: Cryo-MSCs susceptible to T-cell apoptosis in direct co-culture. IFNγ pre-licensing rescues in vitro immunosuppression (IDO-dependent) but does NOT rescue in vivo lung homing defect.
Limitations: Murine in vivo model; limited donor numbers; no clinical translation testing
Clinical Relevance: High – identifies irreversible vs reversible cryoinjury; IFNγ pre-licensing strategy

Tissue Detail: Human bone mesenchymal stem cells (hBMSCs), commercial source
Methodology Detail: AO/PI viability staining, Annexin V/PI apoptosis (flow), comet assay (DNA damage), ROS assay (DCFH-DA), cell cycle (PI flow cytometry), F-actin (phalloidin), qPCR (adipogenic/osteogenic genes), scratch migration assay
Title: Cryopreservation with DMSO affects the DNA integrity, apoptosis, cell cycle and function of human bone mesenchymal stem cells
N Replicates: Triplicate minimum (qPCR); n not stated for viability
Cryoprotectant / Method: 10% DMSO (D10) in standard medium
Post-Thaw Viability (%): Post-thaw immediate: high (similar to fresh); delayed: significant decline over 24–48 h culture
Cell Recovery (%): Not separately calculated
Apoptosis Data: 10–15% apoptosis immediately post freeze-thaw (Annexin V+/PI−). Increases over 24–48 h culture
Cell Cycle: G0/G1 arrest at 24 h post-thaw culture (PI staining, p<0.05 vs fresh)
DNA Damage: Comet assay: ~3.8× more DNA damage/repair (tail moment) vs fresh cells at 48 h post-thaw culture (p<0.01)
Surface Markers: Not reported
Functional Assays: ROS: significantly ↑ in post-thaw cells (p<0.05); F-actin: disorganised, reduced intensity vs fresh; Migration: reduced scratch-wound closure (p<0.05); Differentiation genes: adipogenic (ADIPOQ, FABP4) ↓; osteogenic (RUNX2, COL1A1) ↓ (p<0.05)
Gene Expression: Adipogenic and osteogenic gene expression significantly reduced post-thaw vs fresh (p<0.05)
Key Quantitative Data Points: Apoptosis: 10–15% immediate; DNA damage: 3.8× fold; G0/G1 arrest: statistically significant at 24 h; ROS: significantly elevated
Key Finding: 10% DMSO cryopreservation causes 10–15% immediate apoptosis, 3.8× DNA damage, G0/G1 cell cycle arrest, elevated ROS, and reduced differentiation/migration capacity.
Limitations: No comparison to DMSO-free alternatives; commercial cell source; no in vivo validation
Clinical Relevance: High – quantifies DMSO-mediated cell damage; strong rationale for DMSO reduction/elimination

Tissue Detail: Human BM-derived MSCs (donor-expanded, passage 3–5)
Methodology Detail: Trypan blue viability, flow cytometry (Annexin V/7-AAD, surface markers), IDO activity (HPLC), T-cell proliferation (CFSE), trilineage differentiation
Title: Intracellular delivery of trehalose renders mesenchymal stromal cells viable and immunomodulatory competent after cryopreservation
Cryoprotectant / Method: Intracellular trehalose (electroporation-loaded); compared vs 10% DMSO
Post-Thaw Viability (%): Trehalose group: ~85–90% (comparable to DMSO); DMSO: ~88–92%
Cell Recovery (%): Similar between trehalose and DMSO groups (p>0.05)
Apoptosis Data: Annexin V+: trehalose group comparable to DMSO (p>0.05); both elevated vs non-frozen
Surface Markers: CD73+, CD90+, CD105+ maintained in both trehalose and DMSO groups (>95%)
Functional Assays: IDO activity: DMSO group impaired IFNγ response immediately post-thaw; trehalose group: IDO activity preserved (p<0.05 vs DMSO). Trilineage differentiation: both groups maintained osteogenic, adipogenic, chondrogenic potential
Immunomodulation: T-cell suppression: trehalose group comparable to fresh MSCs; DMSO group attenuated immediately post-thaw (p<0.05)
Gene Expression: Not reported
Key Quantitative Data Points: IDO activity (kynurenine/μg protein): trehalose ~similar to fresh; DMSO ~50% of fresh immediately post-thaw. T-cell proliferation: trehalose group suppressed to ~30% vs ~55% in DMSO group (% T-cell proliferation remaining)
Key Finding: Intracellular trehalose preserves MSC viability, surface markers, and crucially maintains immunomodulatory competence (IDO activity, T-cell suppression) that is impaired with DMSO.
Limitations: Electroporation loading efficiency variable; no in vivo data; limited donor numbers
Clinical Relevance: High – provides DMSO-free alternative preserving full immunomodulatory function

Tissue Detail: Human BM-derived MSCs (research-grade)
Methodology Detail: Seahorse XF analyser (OCR/ECAR), Western blot (mitochondrial proteins), flow cytometry (viability, mitochondrial membrane potential – JC-1)
Title: Cold storage effects on mitochondrial bioenergetics and protein expression in human mesenchymal stromal cells
Cryoprotectant / Method: Cold storage (hypothermic, 4 °C) – NOT cryopreservation; comparison to fresh
Post-Thaw Viability (%): Cold storage: progressive decline – >80% at 24 h, significant decline at 48–72 h (p<0.05)
Cell Recovery (%): Not calculated
Apoptosis Data: JC-1: mitochondrial membrane potential ↓ with extended cold storage (p<0.05)
Surface Markers: Not reported
Functional Assays: Seahorse OCR (basal respiration): ↓ with cold storage duration (p<0.05); maximal respiratory capacity ↓; ECAR (glycolytic flux): relatively preserved initially; mitochondrial protein expression (OXPHOS complexes): altered by cold storage
Gene Expression: Western blot: mitochondrial OXPHOS complex proteins differentially expressed post cold storage
Key Quantitative Data Points: Basal OCR: ~50–60% reduction at 48 h cold storage vs fresh (p<0.05). Mitochondrial membrane potential: significantly depolarised at 48 h. ECAR: maintained at 24 h, reduced at 48 h
Key Finding: Cold storage impairs mitochondrial bioenergetics in a time-dependent manner. Significant OCR decline by 48 h. Mitochondrial protein expression altered. Implications for cold-chain cell logistics.
Limitations: Not cryopreservation per se; limited to cold storage model; no functional (immunomodulation) endpoints
Clinical Relevance: Moderate–High – informs cold-chain logistics for MSC product transport

Tissue Detail: Human adipose tissue-derived MSCs (AT-MSCs), multiple donors
Methodology Detail: Whole genome expression microarray (Affymetrix HuGene 2.0), flow cytometry (tissue factor – CD142), procoagulant activity assay (thrombin generation), in vivo (murine IV infusion model)
Title: Effects of Freeze-Thawing and Intravenous Infusion on Mesenchymal Stromal Cell Gene Expression
Cryoprotectant / Method: 10% DMSO (standard)
Post-Thaw Viability (%): Not reported (study focused on transcriptomics)
Cell Recovery (%): Not reported
Apoptosis Data: Not reported
Surface Markers: Tissue factor (CD142): significantly ↑ on surface of thawed AT-MSCs vs fresh (p<0.05)
Functional Assays: Procoagulant activity: thawed AT-MSCs show significantly ↑ thrombin generation vs fresh (p<0.05). Microarray: freeze-thaw effects on gene expression present but did not exceed inter-donor differences. IV infusion (2 h in murine lungs): major gene expression changes affecting multiple immunological pathways
Immunomodulation: IV infusion activates immunological response pathways in MSCs within 2 h
Gene Expression: Freeze-thaw: altered whole genome expression; effects < inter-donor variation. IV infusion into lungs: >10-fold upregulation of multiple soluble factors (listed in supplementary). Procoagulant TF significantly elevated post-thaw
Key Quantitative Data Points: Differentially expressed genes (freeze-thaw): statistically significant but fewer than inter-donor differences. TF surface expression: significant ↑ post-thaw. Soluble factors: >10 genes with ≥10-fold upregulation in lung-exposed MSCs
Statistical Tests: Microarray differential expression analysis; paired comparisons; p-value corrections applied
Key Finding: Freeze-thaw alters gene expression but less than donor variability. Procoagulant TF significantly elevated post-thaw. IV infusion in lungs causes major immunological gene expression reprogramming within 2 h.
Limitations: AT-MSCs, not BM-MSCs; murine lung model; no clinical endpoints
Clinical Relevance: High – identifies procoagulant risk of thawed MSC infusion; lung microenvironment reprogramming

Tissue Detail: Human BM-derived MSCs (donor-expanded, passage not stated)
Methodology Detail: Dielectrophoresis (DEP): effective conductance (Geff), effective capacitance (Ceff), cytoplasmic conductivity (σcyto), cell radius; resting membrane potential estimation; trypan blue viability
Title: Electrophysiological analysis of mesenchymal stem cells post-cryopreservation highlights the need for a recovery period: implications for cell-based therapies
N Replicates: Multiple measurement replicates per time point
Cryoprotectant / Method: 10% DMSO (and alternatives tested)
Post-Thaw Viability (%): Standard viability: comparable to pre-freeze (trypan blue, ~85–90%); electrophysiology: significantly deviated from pre-freeze state at 0 h and 24 h post-thaw
Cell Recovery (%): Not calculated
Apoptosis Data: Not separately quantified
Surface Markers: Not reported
Functional Assays: DEP parameters: Ceff (membrane capacitance), Geff (membrane conductance), σcyto significantly altered immediately post-thaw vs pre-freeze (p<0.05). Ceff did NOT fully recover to pre-freezing levels even after 3 days. σcyto (resting membrane potential proxy): significantly deviated at 0 h, partial recovery by Day 3. DMSO group: prolonged recovery vs alternative CPA.
Gene Expression: Not reported
Key Quantitative Data Points: MSC electrophysiology deviates significantly from pre-freeze phenotype at 0 h and does not resemble pre-freeze state until Day 3 post-thaw. Cell membrane capacitance: incomplete recovery at Day 3 (p<0.05 vs pre-freeze). Standard viability: no significant difference (trypan blue) – highlights viability underestimates cellular dysfunction.
Statistical Tests: ANOVA, paired comparisons; significance at p<0.05
Key Finding: Electrophysiological analysis reveals MSC dysfunction invisible to trypan blue viability assays. Cells require ≥3 days post-thaw before electrophysiology resembles pre-freeze state. Membrane capacitance incompletely recovers. DMSO prolongs recovery.
Limitations: Novel DEP technique; limited donor numbers; no functional (immunomodulation/differentiation) correlation
Clinical Relevance: High – challenges standard viability-only release criteria; supports longer recovery periods

Tissue Detail: Commercial BM-MSCs and donor-derived BM-MSCs, passages 2/4/8 (MCB/WCB/FP)
Methodology Detail: Trypan blue / NC-200 viability, flow cytometry (surface markers, CFU-F), Seahorse metabolic (implied), qPCR differentiation, karyotype, telomere length, tumorigenicity, secretome (ELISA/multiplex), 3D microcarrier culture
Title: Evaluation of DMSO-free cryopreservation reagent XT-Thrive for establishment of mesenchymal stem cell bank platform
Cryoprotectant / Method: XT-Thrive (DMSO-free) vs CryoStor CS10 (10% DMSO, serum-free)
Pre-Freeze Viability (%): Pre-freeze: comparable between XT-Thrive and CS10 (~95%+)
Post-Thaw Viability (%): Post-thaw 0 h: XT-Thrive ≥ CS10; post-thaw 4 h: both groups high but MCB stage: significant decline regardless of reagent (p<0.05)
Cell Recovery (%): XT-Thrive: superior recovery vs CS10 at later passages; MCB stage lower recovery in both
Apoptosis Data: Not separately quantified
DNA Damage: Genomic integrity: XT-Thrive preserved; CS10 (DMSO) associated with genomic instability signals
Surface Markers: CD73+, CD90+, CD105+ maintained in both; CFU-F efficiency: XT-Thrive > CS10 (p<0.05)
Functional Assays: Proliferation (cPDL): XT-Thrive > CS10 at later passages (p<0.05). Attachment efficiency post-thaw: XT-Thrive superior. Metabolic activity: XT-Thrive superior. Differentiation: both groups maintained trilineage. Secretome: preserved. Karyotype: stable in both. Tumorigenicity: negative in both.
Immunomodulation: Immunomodulatory effects evaluated (details in paper); XT-Thrive comparable/superior to CS10
Gene Expression: Not reported (transcriptomic)
Key Quantitative Data Points: cPDL at FP stage: XT-Thrive significantly higher than CS10 (p<0.05). CFU-F: XT-Thrive > CS10 at all passage stages. MCB viability decline at 4 h post-thaw: both groups (early passage sensitivity). XT-Thrive attachment efficiency: superior to CS10 (p<0.05).
Key Finding: XT-Thrive (DMSO-free) outperforms CS10 in post-thaw recovery, proliferation, clonogenicity, and preserves genomic integrity across MCB/WCB/FP banking stages. Supports clinical-grade DMSO-free banking.
Limitations: Single study group; limited donor numbers for donor-derived cells; 2026 pre-GMP (RUO only)
Clinical Relevance: Very High – directly addresses GMP banking platform development with DMSO-free reagent

Tissue Detail: Human BM stromal cells from multiple centres (n=8 centres, diverse protocols)
Methodology Detail: Flow cytometry (surface markers, apoptosis), CFU-F assay, trilineage differentiation, mixed lymphocyte reaction (MLR), gene expression microarray, colony-forming unit assay
Title: Manufacturing differences affect human bone marrow stromal cell characteristics and function: Comparison of production methods and products from multiple centers
Cryoprotectant / Method: Various (centre-specific DMSO-based, 5–10% DMSO); heterogeneous protocols
Post-Thaw Viability (%): Highly variable between centres (range ~70–95%)
Cell Recovery (%): Highly variable between centres
Apoptosis Data: Apoptosis markers varied significantly between centres
Surface Markers: CD73+, CD90+, CD105+ universally positive; CD31, CD34, CD45, HLA-DR negative across centres. Significant inter-centre variation in CD49a, CD54, CD106, CD166, HLA-ABC expression
Functional Assays: CFU-F: significant inter-centre variation. Immunosuppression (MLR): some centres showed significant suppression; others did not (p<0.05 for inter-centre differences). Trilineage differentiation: variable efficiency
Immunomodulation: Significant inter-centre variation in T-cell suppression capacity (MLR)
Gene Expression: Microarray: substantial inter-centre differences in gene expression profiles; clustering by centre > clustering by passage
Key Quantitative Data Points: CFU-F counts: range 10–85 colonies per 1000 cells across centres. MLR suppression: range 20–80% reduction in proliferation. Surface marker expression: e.g., CD106 ranged from <5% to >80% positive across centres
Statistical Tests: ANOVA, pairwise comparisons; statistical significance p<0.05; clustering analysis
Key Finding: Substantial inter-centre variability in BM-MSC characteristics, function, and gene expression. Manufacturing method is a dominant source of variability. Highlights critical need for standardisation.
Limitations: Retrospective multi-centre design; donors not matched; different passages
Clinical Relevance: Very High – foundational for clinical standardisation of MSC manufacturing

Tissue Detail: Pooled human BM multipotent MSCs (n=5 donors pooled)
Methodology Detail: Trypan blue viability, flow cytometry (surface markers, apoptosis), CFU-F, trilineage differentiation, karyotype, RT-PCR, population doubling time
Title: Comparative cellular and molecular analyses of pooled BM multipotent MSCs during continuous passaging and after successive cryopreservation
Cryoprotectant / Method: 10% DMSO + 90% FBS
Pre-Freeze Viability (%): P3: ~98%; gradual decline with successive passages
Post-Thaw Viability (%): After 1st cryo: ~92–95%; after 3rd cryo: ~85–88%; significant decline with successive cycles (p<0.05)
Cell Recovery (%): Gradual decline with successive cryopreservation cycles
Apoptosis Data: Apoptosis (Annexin V): progressively increased with successive cryo cycles (p<0.05 at 3rd cycle vs 1st)
Surface Markers: CD73+, CD90+, CD105+ maintained through P10 and after successive cryo. CD34−, CD45− maintained throughout.
Functional Assays: CFU-F: maintained through successive cryo cycles. Population doubling time: increased with passage (P3: ~48 h; P10: ~72 h). Trilineage differentiation: maintained through successive cryo. Karyotype: normal diploid through P10.
Gene Expression: RT-PCR: pluripotency genes (OCT4, SOX2, NANOG) detected at low levels; maintained with successive cryo
Key Quantitative Data Points: Viability: P3 1st cryo ~94%; P3 3rd cryo ~87% (p<0.05). Apoptosis rate: 1st cryo ~3%; 3rd cryo ~8% (p<0.05). PDT: ~48 h (P3) to ~72 h (P10). CFU-F: no significant difference across 3 freeze-thaw cycles
Key Finding: Fundamental MSC characteristics maintained through 3 successive cryopreservation cycles. Viability and apoptosis worsen progressively with successive cycles but remain clinically acceptable. Extended passaging increases PDT but not identity.
Limitations: Pooled donors (cannot separate inter-donor variability); 90% FBS not clinically translatable
Clinical Relevance: Moderate–High – relevant for cell banking strategies requiring multiple freeze-thaw cycles

Tissue Detail: Human MSCs from BM and adipose tissue; 7 international centres (USA×5, Australia, Germany)
Methodology Detail: Flow cytometry (viability, surface markers CD45/CD73/CD90/CD105), cell counting (NucleoCounter), global gene expression (microarray/RNA-seq)
Title: Cryopreservation of MSCs using a DMSO-free solution is comparable to DMSO-containing cryoprotectants: international multicenter PACT/BEST study
Cryoprotectant / Method: DMSO-free: SGI (sucrose+glycerol+isoleucine in PlasmaLyte A, Univ. Minnesota); In-house DMSO: 5–10% centre-specific
Freezing Rate: Standardised: −10 °C/min to 0 °C; hold 15 min; −1 °C/min to −8 °C; −50 °C/min to −45 °C; +15 °C/min to −12 °C; −3 °C/min to −100 °C
Thaw Method: 37 °C water bath (until last sliver of ice)
Post-Thaw Viability (%): DMSO in-house: 94.3% − 4.5% = ~89.8% (p=0.049 vs pre-freeze). SGI (DMSO-free): 94.3% − 11.4% = ~82.9% (p<0.001 vs pre-freeze)
Cell Recovery (%): SGI: 92.9% (95% CI: 85.7–100%). DMSO in-house: 92.9% − 5.6% = ~87.3% (p=0.013 vs SGI). SGI recovery HIGHER than DMSO despite lower viability.
Apoptosis Data: Not separately reported
Surface Markers: CD45: <5% (both groups). CD73+, CD90+, CD105+: >95% (both groups). No significant difference between SGI and DMSO (p>0.05)
Functional Assays: Gene expression: no significant global difference between SGI and DMSO groups (PCA clustering)
Gene Expression: Global gene expression (microarray): no significant difference between SGI and DMSO groups
Key Quantitative Data Points: Pre-freeze viability: 94.3% (95% CI 87.2–100%). Post-thaw viability DMSO: ~89.8% (−4.5%, p=0.049). Post-thaw viability SGI: ~82.9% (−11.4%, p<0.001). Recovery SGI: 92.9% (95% CI 85.7–100%). Recovery DMSO: ~87.3% (95% CI ~79–95.3%, lower than SGI, p=0.013). All surface markers >95% in both groups.
Statistical Tests: Mixed linear model; 95% CIs; p<0.05 threshold
Key Finding: DMSO-free SGI solution provides higher cell recovery than DMSO despite slightly lower post-thaw viability. Immunophenotype and gene expression comparable. Clinically acceptable viability (>80%). 7-centre international validation.
Limitations: Post-thaw functional assays not performed; single donor per centre; immunomodulation not assessed
Clinical Relevance: Very High – highest-quality evidence for DMSO-free MSC cryopreservation; multi-centre validation

Tissue Detail: Human BM-MSCs (GMP-grade, clinical translation focus)
Methodology Detail: Trypan blue viability, flow cytometry (surface markers, apoptosis), osteogenic differentiation assay, immunosuppression (MLR), IDO activity, in vitro bone repair model
Title: Directly Thawed Bone Marrow Mesenchymal Stromal Cells Retain Mechanism of Action and Support Bone Clinical Translation
Cryoprotectant / Method: Standard DMSO-based (GMP-grade)
Post-Thaw Viability (%): Directly thawed: ~85–92% (comparable to recovery period control)
Cell Recovery (%): Maintained in directly thawed group
Apoptosis Data: Not significantly different between directly thawed and recovery period groups
Surface Markers: CD73+, CD90+, CD105+ maintained; HLA-DR expression assessed (immunogenicity profiling)
Functional Assays: Osteogenic differentiation: directly thawed MSCs retained full osteogenic capacity; bone nodule formation comparable to culture-recovered cells (p>0.05). IDO activity: maintained in directly thawed cells post IFNγ stimulation. In vitro bone repair: directly thawed cells supported mineralisation comparable to fresh/recovered cells.
Immunomodulation: MLR suppression: directly thawed MSCs suppressed T-cell proliferation comparable to recovery period cells (p>0.05). IDO induction intact.
Gene Expression: Not reported
Key Quantitative Data Points: Osteogenic mineralisation (Alizarin Red): no significant difference directly thawed vs recovery period (p>0.05). T-cell suppression: ~60–70% in directly thawed vs ~65% recovery period (NS). IDO activity: maintained in both groups
Key Finding: Directly thawed BM-MSCs (no recovery period) retain full therapeutic mechanism of action for bone repair. Challenges assumption that recovery period is always required. Key for 'off-the-shelf' bone clinical translation.
Limitations: Single indication (bone repair); GMP-grade product (may not extrapolate to all settings); 2026 study (preliminary external validation needed)
Clinical Relevance: Very High – supports direct clinical use of cryo-MSCs without mandatory recovery period for bone indications

Tissue Detail: Human BM-MSCs (n=7 samples, 5 donors) and Cord Tissue MSCs (CT-MSCs, n=6 samples, 5 donors)
Methodology Detail: 10x Genomics Chromium droplet-based scRNA-seq; SC3 clustering; Seurat differential expression; GSEA; cell cycle scoring
Title: Single-cell RNA-seq of out-of-thaw mesenchymal stromal cells shows tissue-of-origin differences and inter-donor cell-cycle variations
Cryoprotectant / Method: Controlled-rate freezing; standard DMSO-based
Post-Thaw Viability (%): Not reported (scRNA-seq on viable cells)
Cell Recovery (%): Not calculated
Apoptosis Data: Not separately quantified (transcriptomic study)
Cell Cycle: Cell cycle scoring (Seurat): significant inter-donor variability in S/G2M fraction. BM-MSCs: higher proportion G1-arrested cells; CT-MSCs: more cells in S/G2M phase (p<0.05 inter-tissue)
Surface Markers: Not assessed (scRNA-seq)
Functional Assays: Transcriptomic: gene-set enrichment analysis (GSEA) – BM-MSCs: enriched for immunomodulatory/senescence pathways; CT-MSCs: enriched for cell proliferation/ECM pathways. Tissue-of-origin: dominant source of transcriptomic variance (> donor variance)
Immunomodulation: BM-MSCs: immune-regulatory gene signature enriched. CT-MSCs: less immunomodulatory, more proliferative signature.
Gene Expression: BM vs CT: significantly different expression profiles (1000s of DEGs). Key BM-up: immunomodulatory genes (IDO1, CXCL9, IL6). Key CT-up: proliferative/ECM genes (MKI67, TOP2A, COL1A1). Donor variance < tissue variance.
Key Quantitative Data Points: BM-MSCs cells profiled: Table 1 (average cells/sample: ~2000–4000; average genes/cell: ~2000–3000 UMI). CT-MSC cells: Table 2 (similar metrics). Tissue-of-origin explains >60% transcriptomic variance (PCA)
Statistical Tests: Wilcoxon rank sum test; FDR-adjusted p-value <0.05; negative-log p-values
Key Finding: Tissue-of-origin dominates transcriptomic heterogeneity of out-of-thaw MSCs (> donor variability). BM-MSCs: immunomodulatory phenotype. CT-MSCs: proliferative phenotype. Inter-donor cell cycle variability significant – has implications for immunomodulatory vs proliferative potency.
Limitations: Only two tissue sources compared; no functional correlation; viability/recovery not characterised
Clinical Relevance: High – informs tissue source selection for specific clinical indications; characterises out-of-thaw transcriptome

Tissue Detail: H9-MSC cell line (human ESC-derived) and primary BM-MSCs
Methodology Detail: Post-thaw recovery (NucleoCounter), attachment efficiency, actin cytoskeleton (phalloidin/imaging), trilineage differentiation, RNA-seq, qRT-PCR, DNA hydroxymethylation (dot blot)
Title: Improved Post-Thaw Function and Epigenetic Changes in MSCs Cryopreserved Using Multicomponent Osmolyte Solutions
N Donors: H9-MSC cell line + primary (n=3 biological triplicates)
Cryoprotectant / Method: DMSO-free osmolyte solutions tested: SMC (sucrose+mannitol+creatine), SGC (sucrose+glycerol+creatine), SGI (sucrose+glycerol+isoleucine). Compared vs 10% DMSO in FBS.
Cell Recovery (%): SGI: comparable to DMSO (p>0.05). SGC: comparable (p>0.05). SMC: significantly lower (p<0.05 vs DMSO). Iterative optimisation showed 'sweet spot' for osmolyte combinations.
Apoptosis Data: Not separately quantified
Surface Markers: Not assessed (cell line study)
Functional Assays: Actin cytoskeleton: osmolyte solutions showed more normal alignment vs DMSO (disrupted actin in DMSO). Attachment efficiency: SGI and SGC superior to DMSO (p<0.05). Osteogenic/chondrogenic differentiation: maintained in all groups. RNA-seq: osmolyte-frozen cells clustered separately from DMSO and fresh; upregulated cytoprotective genes (BCL2, VCAM1, ICAM1, galanin) in osmolyte vs DMSO groups.
Gene Expression: RNA-seq: DMSO and fresh cluster together; osmolyte solutions form distinct cluster. Osmolyte-frozen: ↑BCL2 (anti-apoptotic), ↑VCAM1/ICAM1 (adhesion), ↑galanin (osmotic regulator). DNA hydroxymethylation: SGI = DMSO (ns); SMC: significantly different (p<0.05 vs fresh control)
Key Quantitative Data Points: Recovery: SGI ~80%, SGC ~78%, SMC ~70%, DMSO ~87% (p>0.05 for SGI and SGC vs DMSO). Attachment: osmolytes 20–30% higher than DMSO (p<0.05). BCL2 fold-change: ~2–3× vs DMSO. DNA hydroxymethylation: SMC only significantly different
Statistical Tests: ANOVA; pairwise Tukey; p<0.05; RNA-seq: FDR-adjusted p-value
Key Finding: Multicomponent osmolyte solutions (particularly SGI) provide comparable recovery to DMSO with improved post-thaw attachment, actin cytoskeleton preservation, and upregulation of cytoprotective gene expression. Epigenetically favourable vs DMSO.
Limitations: Cell line (H9-MSC) dominant; limited immunomodulation testing; no in vivo validation
Clinical Relevance: High – SGI solution basis for Mamo 2024 multi-centre validation; foundation of DMSO-free cryopreservation platforms

Tissue Detail: Human BM-MSCs on surface-engineered microcarriers (HS-COL bilayer)
Methodology Detail: Trypan blue viability (on-carrier and off-carrier), phase contrast microscopy, flow cytometry (surface markers), attachment efficiency, post-thaw expansion capacity, metabolic activity (resazurin)
Title: Cryogenic microcarrier-assisted stem cell storage (Cryo-MASCS): a preservation method enabling scalable culture of human mesenchymal stem cells
Cryoprotectant / Method: 10% DMSO + 10% FBS (DMSO group); serum-free 2% BSA + bFGF 20 ng/mL (SF group) – both on-carrier
Freezing Rate: Overnight (>12 h) in liquid nitrogen tank
Post-Thaw Viability (%): DMSO on-carrier: high recovery without intermediate processing; SF on-carrier: comparable viability
Cell Recovery (%): On-carrier (Cryo-MASCS): significantly improved vs standard suspension cryo (no carrier wash step needed)
Apoptosis Data: Not quantified
Surface Markers: CD73+, CD90+, CD105+ maintained post-thaw on carrier
Functional Assays: Post-thaw expansion: cells directly returned to suspension culture on carriers without processing; attachment and expansion confirmed. Metabolic activity: comparable between DMSO and SF Cryo-MASCS. Scalability: system eliminates post-thaw wash step, reducing cell losses. Seeding density optimised.
Gene Expression: Not reported
Key Quantitative Data Points: System eliminates post-thaw wash/centrifugation step. On-carrier cell viability comparable between DMSO and SF groups (p>0.05). Expansion kinetics post-thaw: maintained on microcarrier substrate without intermediate culture step.
Key Finding: Cryo-MASCS integrates cell attachment, cryopreservation, and thawing directly on microcarriers. Eliminates post-thaw processing steps. Enables scalable MSC manufacturing directly post-thaw. Compatible with serum-free DMSO-free conditions.
Limitations: Novel platform; limited donor numbers; no immunomodulation or in vivo data; manufacturing scalability not fully validated
Clinical Relevance: High – addresses scalable GMP manufacturing challenge; reduces cell losses during post-thaw handling

Tissue Detail: Human Wharton's Jelly MSCs (WJ-MSCs) from cord tissue
Methodology Detail: Trypan blue viability, AO/PI staining, flow cytometry (surface markers, apoptosis), trilineage differentiation, proliferation (PDT), CFU-F
Title: Trehalose Cryopreservation of Human Mesenchymal Stem Cells from Cord Tissue
Cryoprotectant / Method: Trehalose-based (extracellular; concentration optimised); compared vs 10% DMSO
Post-Thaw Viability (%): Trehalose (optimal): ~82–88%; DMSO: ~88–92%. Trehalose slightly lower but not significant at optimal concentration (p>0.05)
Cell Recovery (%): Trehalose: comparable to DMSO at optimised concentration (p>0.05)
Apoptosis Data: Trehalose: low apoptosis comparable to DMSO (p>0.05) at optimal concentration
Surface Markers: CD73+, CD90+, CD105+ maintained in both trehalose and DMSO groups (>90%)
Functional Assays: Trilineage differentiation: maintained in trehalose group (comparable to DMSO). CFU-F: comparable between groups (p>0.05). PDT: no significant difference between trehalose and DMSO groups.
Gene Expression: Not reported
Key Quantitative Data Points: Post-thaw viability at optimal trehalose: ~85% vs DMSO ~90% (ns at p>0.05). Recovery comparable. CFU-F: ~12–15 colonies/100 cells (both groups). Differentiation: comparable histological scores.
Key Finding: Trehalose-based cryopreservation is viable for cord tissue WJ-MSCs with post-thaw viability, differentiation, and clonogenicity comparable to DMSO at optimised concentration. Provides DMSO-free option for perinatal MSC banking.
Limitations: WJ-specific; optimal trehalose concentration needs validation in GMP setting; no immunomodulation data
Clinical Relevance: High – DMSO-free option for perinatal MSC banking; relevant to cord blood banking facilities

Tissue Detail: Placental mesenchymal stromal cells (human placenta; post-partum)
Methodology Detail: RNA-seq (transcriptomic profiling), flow cytometry (surface markers, apoptosis), ELISA (growth factors), Western blot, qRT-PCR
Title: Effects of human placenta cryopreservation on molecular characteristics of placental mesenchymal stromal cells
Cryoprotectant / Method: Tissue-level cryopreservation (placenta tissue frozen, not cell suspension); DMSO-based tissue CPA
Thaw Method: Controlled thaw; MSCs then isolated from thawed tissue
Post-Thaw Viability (%): MSCs from cryo-tissue: slightly reduced viability vs fresh-isolated (p<0.05 at some time points)
Cell Recovery (%): Comparable number of MSCs isolated from cryo-tissue vs fresh
Apoptosis Data: Slightly elevated apoptotic markers in MSCs from cryo-placenta (p<0.05)
Surface Markers: CD73+, CD90+, CD105+ maintained; minor differences in some markers after placental cryopreservation
Functional Assays: Growth factor secretion (VEGF, HGF, FGF): altered by placental cryopreservation. qRT-PCR: significant changes in molecular marker expression
Gene Expression: RNA-seq: significant changes in gene expression of MSCs from cryopreserved placenta vs fresh placenta. Key pathways: stress response, ECM, apoptosis pathways altered.
Key Quantitative Data Points: DEGs (cryo-tissue vs fresh-tissue derived MSCs): hundreds of significantly altered genes. VEGF: significantly reduced in MSCs from cryo-tissue (p<0.05). Select surface markers: minor but significant variation.
Statistical Tests: DESeq2; FDR-adjusted p<0.05; t-test for individual gene validation
Key Finding: Placental tissue cryopreservation significantly alters molecular characteristics of subsequently isolated MSCs. Stress response genes upregulated; secretome altered. Impacts on therapeutic potential should be evaluated.
Limitations: Tissue cryo (not cell suspension); specific to placental MSCs; limited functional data
Clinical Relevance: Moderate – relevant to placental tissue banking; informs MSC quality from cryo-stored placenta

Tissue Detail: Placental multipotent stromal cells (human placenta, chorionic villi origin)
Methodology Detail: AO/PI viability, flow cytometry (apoptosis, surface markers), trilineage differentiation, GFP transgene expression (fluorescence), controlled-rate freezer with temperature monitoring
Title: Influence of temperature fluctuations during cryopreservation on vital parameters, differentiation potential, and transgene expression of placental multipotent stromal cells
Cryoprotectant / Method: 10% DMSO + 90% FBS; controlled conditions with/without temperature fluctuations
Freezing Rate: −1 °C/min standard; intentional temperature fluctuation conditions tested (±5 °C at nucleation)
Post-Thaw Viability (%): Optimal (no fluctuations): ~90–95%. Temperature fluctuations: significantly reduced to ~70–80% (p<0.05). Repeated fluctuations: further reduction to <70%
Cell Recovery (%): Proportional to viability; fluctuations significantly reduced recovery (p<0.05)
Apoptosis Data: Apoptosis significantly increased with temperature fluctuations vs optimal cryo (p<0.05). Necrosis also increased.
Surface Markers: CD73+, CD90+, CD105+ maintained in optimal group; slight but significant reduction in fluctuation groups (p<0.05)
Functional Assays: Trilineage differentiation: maintained in optimal group; impaired in high-fluctuation group (adipogenesis most sensitive). Transgene (GFP) expression: significantly reduced after temperature fluctuations (p<0.05 vs optimal)
Gene Expression: GFP transgene expression level used as functional readout
Key Quantitative Data Points: Viability: optimal ~93%; 1 fluctuation ~79%; 3 fluctuations ~65% (p<0.05 at each step). GFP expression: ~100% (optimal) → ~80% (1 fluctuation) → ~55% (3 fluctuations, p<0.05). Apoptosis: ~3% optimal; ~12% fluctuation (p<0.05).
Key Finding: Temperature fluctuations during cryopreservation (even ±5°C transient warming) significantly impair placental MSC viability, differentiation potential, and transgene expression. Strict temperature control throughout freezing/storage critical.
Limitations: Placental MSCs only; transgene expression as proxy measure; no clinical endpoint
Clinical Relevance: High – highlights critical need for temperature monitoring in cryopreservation logistics

Tissue Detail: Tonsil-derived MSCs (T-MSCs); human tonsil tissue
Methodology Detail: Trypan blue viability, flow cytometry (surface markers, apoptosis), trilineage differentiation, BrdU proliferation, SA-β-gal senescence, karyotype, population doubling time
Title: Effects of donor age, long-term passage culture, and cryopreservation on tonsil-derived mesenchymal stem cells
Cryoprotectant / Method: 10% DMSO + 90% FBS
Post-Thaw Viability (%): Post cryo: ~89–93% across donor ages and passages (no significant age effect on post-thaw viability)
Cell Recovery (%): Maintained across age groups and passages (p>0.05)
Apoptosis Data: Apoptosis: slightly elevated post-cryo but comparable across age groups (p>0.05 for age effect)
Cell Cycle: Proliferation: reduced with advancing passage regardless of cryopreservation; donor age effect significant at high passage
Surface Markers: CD73+, CD90+, CD105+ maintained through P10 and after cryo. CD34−, CD45− maintained. No significant age effect on phenotype.
Functional Assays: BrdU proliferation: passage-dependent reduction (p<0.05 by P8+); donor age effect at later passages (p<0.05). SA-β-gal senescence: increased with passage (p<0.05 at P8+); no significant cryo effect on senescence. Trilineage differentiation: maintained through P10 after cryo. Karyotype: normal diploid maintained. PDT: increased with passage (~36 h P3 → ~60 h P10, p<0.05)
Gene Expression: Not reported
Key Quantitative Data Points: Post-thaw viability: ~91% (consistent across ages). Senescence (SA-β-gal+): ~3% (P3) → ~18% (P10), p<0.05. PDT: ~36 h (P3) → ~60 h (P10), p<0.05. Proliferation (BrdU index): ~85% (P3) → ~55% (P10, elderly donors, p<0.05 vs young).
Key Finding: Cryopreservation per se does not significantly impair tonsil-derived MSC viability or characteristics. Passage number and donor age are dominant factors affecting proliferative capacity. Senescence increases with passage regardless of cryopreservation.
Limitations: Tonsil-specific; 90% FBS cryomedium; no functional immunomodulation assays
Clinical Relevance: Moderate – relevant to selection of passage number and donor age for T-MSC banking

Tissue Detail: Perinatal tissue MSCs (cord, placenta, amniotic) vs adult BM-MSCs
Methodology Detail: Bulk RNA-seq transcriptomic analysis, bioinformatic pathway enrichment, PCA, hierarchical clustering, cross-dataset meta-analysis
Title: Strong Transcriptional Signature of MSCs from Perinatal Tissues vs Adult Bone Marrow Independent of Donors or Culture Conditions
Cryoprotectant / Method: N/A (transcriptomic comparative study; cryopreservation details not primary focus)
Post-Thaw Viability (%): N/A
Cell Recovery (%): N/A
Apoptosis Data: N/A
Surface Markers: N/A
Functional Assays: Transcriptomic: perinatal MSCs robustly distinguished from BM-MSCs across donors, culture conditions, and datasets by gene signature
Immunomodulation: BM-MSCs: immunomodulatory gene signature enriched. Perinatal MSCs: proliferative/developmental gene signature enriched.
Gene Expression: Robust tissue-of-origin transcriptional signatures independent of donor variability or culture conditions. Key perinatal-enriched genes: HOXA/B family, developmental transcription factors. Key BM-enriched: immune genes, adipogenic markers.
Key Quantitative Data Points: PCA: tissue-of-origin accounts for dominant variance (>60%). Perinatal vs BM: 1000s of DEGs (FDR<0.05). Tissue signature robust across independent datasets (>5 datasets).
Statistical Tests: DESeq2; limma; FDR-adjusted p<0.05; PCA; hierarchical clustering
Key Finding: Perinatal tissue MSCs have a strong, reproducible transcriptional signature distinguishing them from adult BM-MSCs, independent of donor or culture conditions. This has fundamental implications for clinical indication selection and product characterisation.
Limitations: No functional validation of transcriptomic differences in cryopreservation context; retrospective meta-analysis
Clinical Relevance: High – defines transcriptional basis for tissue source selection in MSC therapy

Tissue Detail: Human umbilical cord mesenchymal stem cells (UC-MSCs)
Methodology Detail: Cold storage (4 °C) in Celsior solution ± quercetin; trypan blue viability, flow cytometry (apoptosis, surface markers), trilineage differentiation, proliferation assay
Title: Quercetin enhances the cold storage effect on human umbilical cord mesenchymal stem cells in Celsior solution
Cryoprotectant / Method: Celsior solution ± quercetin (cold storage, 4 °C)
Post-Thaw Viability (%): Quercetin + Celsior: viability maintained vs Celsior alone; significant improvement at extended storage timepoints (p<0.05)
Cell Recovery (%): Higher in quercetin group at extended cold storage duration (p<0.05)
Apoptosis Data: Quercetin significantly reduced apoptosis vs Celsior alone (p<0.05); Annexin V/PI staining
Surface Markers: CD73+, CD90+, CD105+ maintained across groups (p>0.05)
Functional Assays: Trilineage differentiation maintained; proliferation capacity preserved
Gene Expression: Not reported
Key Quantitative Data Points: Quercetin supplementation significantly improved post-cold-storage viability and reduced apoptosis (p<0.05)
Key Finding: Quercetin supplementation enhances cold (4 °C) storage of UC-MSCs in Celsior solution, reducing apoptosis and maintaining viability, surface markers, and differentiation capacity. Supports DMSO-free, ambient cold transport strategies.
Limitations: Cold storage model (not cryogenic freeze-thaw); UC-MSC source only; no in vivo validation; quercetin concentration optimisation not fully explored
Clinical Relevance: High – applicable to MSC logistics and point-of-care delivery where cryogenic storage is unavailable

Tissue Detail: Human MSCs (bone marrow and adipose-derived); expansion and licensing conditions
Methodology Detail: Transcriptome profiling across expansion (serial passage) and licensing (IFNγ, TGF-β); validation of MSC lineage gene signatures; surface marker analysis
Title: Transcriptome profiles acquired during cell expansion and licensing validate mesenchymal stromal cell lineage genes
Cryoprotectant / Method: Standard culture expansion; IFNγ/TGF-β licensing (not primary cryo variable)
Post-Thaw Viability (%): Not the primary focus
Cell Recovery (%): Not reported
Apoptosis Data: Not reported
Surface Markers: CD73, CD90, CD105 stable across expansion; licensing alters secretory profile not surface phenotype
Functional Assays: Lineage gene signatures validated; licensing induces distinct transcriptome states without ablating MSC identity
Immunomodulation: IFNγ-licensed MSCs show upregulated immunosuppressive transcriptome; IDO1, CXCL9, CXCL10 upregulated
Gene Expression: Distinct transcriptome clusters by expansion passage and licensing; core MSC lineage genes stable; IDO1, CXCL9, CXCL10 upregulated by IFNγ
Key Quantitative Data Points: Transcriptome clustering validates passage-dependent and licensing-dependent expression states; specific DEG counts passage-dependent
Statistical Tests: Differential gene expression analysis; clustering; FDR-corrected p-values
Key Finding: Transcriptome profiling validates a core MSC lineage gene signature stable across expansion and licensing. Licensing (IFNγ) induces functionally relevant shifts (IDO1, CXCL9/10) without MSC identity loss. Supports transcriptomics as a potency characterisation tool.
Limitations: Non-cryopreservation primary focus; in vitro licensing may not replicate in vivo priming; donor n uncertain
Clinical Relevance: High – transcriptome-based potency characterisation relevant to ATMP release criteria; supports pre-infusion licensing strategies

Tissue Detail: Human umbilical cord tissue (Wharton's Jelly ECM) as 3D storage niche for MSCs
Methodology Detail: Conditioned decellularised umbilical cord tissue as 3D ECM scaffold; MSC seeding and in vitro storage; flow cytometry, differentiation, proliferation, viability assessment
Title: Conditioned umbilical cord tissue provides a natural three-dimensional storage compartment as in vitro stem cell niche for human mesenchymal stroma/stem cells
Cryoprotectant / Method: Decellularised umbilical cord ECM scaffold (3D niche); no conventional CPA
Post-Thaw Viability (%): MSCs in 3D niche maintained higher viability vs 2D control (p<0.05)
Cell Recovery (%): Recovery from 3D niche comparable to 2D culture; niche supports proliferation
Apoptosis Data: Reduced apoptosis in 3D niche vs 2D monolayer (p<0.05)
Surface Markers: CD73+, CD90+, CD105+ maintained; no phenotypic drift in 3D niche
Functional Assays: Trilineage differentiation maintained; proliferation comparable to 2D; niche supports stemness
Gene Expression: Stemness genes maintained (not primary variable)
Key Quantitative Data Points: Viability in 3D niche significantly higher at extended timepoints (p<0.05); apoptosis reduced; markers maintained
Key Finding: Conditioned decellularised umbilical cord tissue provides a natural 3D ECM microenvironment maintaining MSC viability, phenotype, and differentiation. Novel storage paradigm preserving niche architecture without DMSO or cryogenic infrastructure.
Limitations: In vitro model only; decellularisation protocol variability; no direct comparison with DMSO cryopreservation; no in vivo data
Clinical Relevance: Moderate–High – scaffold-based storage with potential for ATMP manufacturing; eliminates DMSO and cryogenic requirements for short-term storage

Tissue Detail: Human amniotic fluid-derived mesenchymal stem cells (AF-MSCs)
Methodology Detail: Replicative senescence model (serial passaging P3–P10+); SA-β-galactosidase senescence assay, flow cytometry (surface markers, apoptosis), T-cell suppression, cytokine profiling
Title: Replicative senescence in amniotic fluid-derived mesenchymal stem cells and its impact on their immunomodulatory properties
Cryoprotectant / Method: Replicative senescence (serial passage); standard culture/banking intervals
Post-Thaw Viability (%): ~88% (P7), ~78% (P10+); significant decline vs P3 (p<0.05)
Cell Recovery (%): Reduced at high passage; PDT increased ~40% by P10+ (p<0.05)
Apoptosis Data: Progressive increase with passage (p<0.05); Annexin V/PI staining
Cell Cycle: G1 arrest increases at high passage; proliferative index declines (p<0.05)
Surface Markers: CD73, CD90, CD105 maintained through P7; CD105↓ at P10+ (p<0.05)
Functional Assays: T-cell suppression: ~65% (P3) → ~35% (P10+), p<0.001; proliferation index declines
Immunomodulation: IDO, PGE2, IL-10 secretion progressively reduced; IFNγ responsiveness attenuated at P10+
Gene Expression: Not reported (protein-level assays)
Key Quantitative Data Points: SA-β-gal+: ~4% (P3) → ~22% (P10+), p<0.001. T-cell suppression: ~65% (P3) → ~35% (P10+), p<0.001. PDT increased ~40% by P10+.
Key Finding: Replicative senescence in AF-MSCs progressively impairs immunomodulatory potency, surface marker fidelity, and proliferative capacity. High-passage cells retain MSC phenotype but lose functional potency — directly informs passage-limit policies in ATMP manufacturing.
Limitations: Senescence model (not cryogenic); AF-MSC source not universally representative; in vitro only; donor variability not fully characterised
Clinical Relevance: High – passage-dependent potency loss informs ATMP release criteria, GMP passage limits, and functional potency assay requirements for cell therapy supply chains

Tissue Detail: Human bone marrow-derived MSCs (BM-MSCs); cytokine licensing model (IFNγ)
Methodology Detail: Chromatin immunoprecipitation (ChIP) assay at IDO1 promoter; bisulphite sequencing (DNA methylation); qPCR (IDO1, CXCL10 mRNA); ELISA (IDO enzymatic activity); flow cytometry (surface markers)
Title: In vitro cytokine licensing induces persistent permissive chromatin at the Indoleamine 2,3-dioxygenase promoter
N Donors: Multiple BM-MSC donors (n not specified in abstract)
N Replicates: Multiple independent ChIP and qPCR experiments
Cryoprotectant / Method: IFNγ cytokine licensing (24–72 h); epigenetic mark persistence assessed post-licensing; not a cryopreservation primary study
Post-Thaw Viability (%): Not the primary focus
Cell Recovery (%): Not reported
Apoptosis Data: Not reported
Surface Markers: Standard MSC phenotype maintained (CD73+, CD90+, CD105+); IFNγ licensing upregulates PD-L1 (CD274) and HLA-DR transiently
Functional Assays: IDO enzymatic activity: significantly elevated post-IFNγ licensing (p<0.05); persists ≥72 h post-cytokine withdrawal if permissive chromatin established. CXCL10 mRNA: upregulated by IFNγ, partially retained post-withdrawal.
Immunomodulation: Persistent IDO-mediated immunosuppressive capacity following IFNγ licensing is epigenetically maintained. Permissive chromatin at IDO1 promoter (H3K4me3, reduced DNA methylation) allows rapid IDO re-induction upon re-stimulation.
Gene Expression: IDO1 and CXCL10 mRNA: induced by IFNγ; IDO1 expression persistence correlates with H3K4me3 enrichment at promoter. Bisulphite sequencing: reduced CpG methylation at IDO1 promoter post-licensing.
Key Quantitative Data Points: H3K4me3 enrichment at IDO1 promoter: significantly elevated in IFNγ-licensed vs unlicensed MSCs (p<0.05). IDO activity (kynurenine production): >3-fold increase post-licensing; partially retained 72 h post-cytokine withdrawal. CpG methylation at IDO1 promoter: reduced post-licensing.
Statistical Tests: Student t-test; ANOVA where applicable; p<0.05
Key Finding: IFNγ licensing induces persistent permissive chromatin remodelling (H3K4me3, reduced DNA methylation) at the IDO1 promoter in BM-MSCs. This epigenetic memory enables sustained and rapidly re-inducible immunosuppressive capacity, providing mechanistic basis for pre-infusion licensing strategies in ATMP manufacture.
Limitations: In vitro only; BM-MSC source; duration of epigenetic persistence in vivo unknown; no clinical endpoint data; single cytokine (IFNγ) licensing model
Clinical Relevance: High – provides mechanistic justification for IFNγ pre-licensing protocols in ATMP manufacture; epigenetic memory of IDO1 supports potency retention after cryopreservation and thaw if licensing applied pre-freeze

Tissue Detail: Human Wharton's Jelly MSCs (WJ-MSCs) post-chondrogenic differentiation; immunological characterisation
Methodology Detail: Chondrogenic differentiation (pellet culture, TGF-β3); flow cytometry (surface markers, HLA, PD-L1, PD-L2); T-cell co-culture (mixed lymphocyte reaction, MLR); IDO activity (kynurenine ELISA); IFNγ stimulation
Title: Are the immune properties of mesenchymal stem cells from Wharton's Jelly maintained during chondrogenic differentiation?
N Replicates: Multiple independent differentiation and co-culture experiments
Cryoprotectant / Method: Chondrogenic differentiation (TGF-β3 pellet culture 21 days) as primary variable; cryopreservation not the primary focus but MSC banking context implied
Thaw Method: 37 °C water bath; cells thawed and expanded prior to chondrogenic induction
Post-Thaw Viability (%): Not the primary focus (functional immune assessment post-differentiation)
Cell Recovery (%): Not reported
Apoptosis Data: Not reported
Surface Markers: WJ-MSC phenotype (CD73+, CD90+, CD105+): maintained pre-differentiation. Post-chondrogenic differentiation: downregulation of HLA-I (p<0.05); HLA-II remains low. PD-L1/PD-L2: retained and inducible by IFNγ post-differentiation.
Functional Assays: T-cell suppression (MLR): WJ-MSCs maintain significant T-cell inhibition post-chondrogenic differentiation (p<0.05 vs no-MSC control). IDO activity: present and IFNγ-inducible post-differentiation. Chondrogenic pellets: collagen II, aggrecan expression confirmed.
Immunomodulation: Immunosuppressive capacity (T-cell suppression, IDO activity, PD-L1/PD-L2 expression) is substantially retained after chondrogenic differentiation of WJ-MSCs. IFNγ licensing post-differentiation further enhances IDO-dependent suppression.
Gene Expression: Chondrogenic markers confirmed (COL2A1, ACAN). IDO1 mRNA inducible by IFNγ post-differentiation.
Key Quantitative Data Points: T-cell suppression: ~55–65% in undifferentiated WJ-MSCs; ~45–60% retained post-chondrogenic differentiation (not significantly different, p>0.05). IDO kynurenine: IFNγ-induced IDO activity maintained post-differentiation. HLA-I: significantly reduced post-differentiation (p<0.05).
Key Finding: Wharton's Jelly MSC immune properties (T-cell suppression, IDO activity, PD-L1/PD-L2 expression) are substantially maintained after chondrogenic differentiation. Differentiated WJ-MSCs retain IFNγ-responsive immunosuppressive capacity, supporting immune-privileged status of chondrogenically differentiated cells for cartilage repair applications.
Limitations: In vitro MLR model; WJ-MSC source only; chondrogenic pellet model (2D co-culture may not replicate 3D in vivo environment); no in vivo immune rejection data; donor n limited
Clinical Relevance: High – supports clinical use of allogeneic WJ-MSC-derived chondrocytes in cartilage repair without immunosuppressive co-therapy; relevant to off-the-shelf ATMP banking strategies for orthopaedic indications

Tissue Detail: Human Wharton's Jelly-derived MSCs (WJ-MSCs); controlled rate freezing protocol validation
Methodology Detail: Controlled-rate freezing (programmable rate freezer, −1 °C/min); trypan blue viability, AO/PI staining, flow cytometry (surface markers, apoptosis, Annexin V/PI), trilineage differentiation (osteogenic, adipogenic, chondrogenic), proliferation (PDT)
Title: Cryopreservation of Human Wharton's Jelly-derived Mesenchymal Stem Cells Following Controlled Rate Freezing Protocol
Cryoprotectant / Method: 10% DMSO in serum-containing medium; controlled-rate freezing (−1 °C/min); ± serum-free formulations tested
Post-Thaw Viability (%): ~85–92% (trypan blue / AO-PI); controlled-rate protocol maintained viability vs uncontrolled slow freeze (p<0.05)
Cell Recovery (%): ~75–88% total viable cell recovery post-thaw; controlled-rate superior to uncontrolled (p<0.05)
Apoptosis Data: Annexin V+/PI−: ~8–12% early apoptosis post-thaw; significantly lower with controlled-rate vs uncontrolled (p<0.05)
Surface Markers: CD73+, CD90+, CD105+ maintained post-thaw (>90%); CD45−, CD34− confirmed; no significant change vs pre-freeze (p>0.05)
Functional Assays: Trilineage differentiation (osteogenic, adipogenic, chondrogenic): maintained post-thaw with controlled-rate protocol. PDT: comparable to fresh pre-freeze WJ-MSCs (p>0.05). Attachment efficiency: maintained.
Gene Expression: Not reported
Key Quantitative Data Points: Post-thaw viability: ~88% (controlled-rate) vs ~72% (uncontrolled), p<0.05. Recovery: ~82% (controlled-rate) vs ~65% (uncontrolled), p<0.05. Apoptosis: ~10% (controlled) vs ~22% (uncontrolled), p<0.05.
Key Finding: Controlled-rate freezing (−1 °C/min) significantly outperforms uncontrolled slow freezing for WJ-MSC cryopreservation, achieving higher post-thaw viability (~88%), better cell recovery (~82%), and lower apoptosis (~10%). Standard phenotype and trilineage differentiation capacity fully maintained.
Limitations: WJ-MSC source only; serum-containing medium (not GMP-compliant without modification); immunofunctional potency not assessed; single-centre; donor n limited
Clinical Relevance: High – directly applicable to GMP WJ-MSC banking protocols; validates controlled-rate freezing as the standard for cord tissue MSC cryopreservation in ATMP supply chains

Tissue Detail: Human MSCs from multiple tissue sources (BM, adipose, cord); 7 manufacturing laboratories with shared source material
Methodology Detail: Multi-laboratory comparative study; flow cytometry (surface markers, HLA), trilineage differentiation, functional assays (T-cell suppression, IDO, CFU-F), metabolomics, gene expression; shared source material distributed to 7 sites
Title: Human Mesenchymal Stromal Cell (MSC) Characteristics Vary Among Laboratories When Manufactured From the Same Source Material
Journal: Frontiers in Cell and Developmental Biology
N Donors: Shared source material from single BM donor distributed to 7 independent laboratories
N Replicates: 7 independent manufacturing sites; replicate cultures per site
Cryoprotectant / Method: Site-specific GMP manufacturing protocols (variable CPA, medium, freeze rate, passage); identical starting material
Freezing Rate: Variable by site (each site used own SOP)
Post-Thaw Viability (%): Variable by site: ~75–95%; significant inter-laboratory variation (p<0.05)
Cell Recovery (%): Significant inter-laboratory variation in expansion fold-change and viable cell yield (p<0.05)
Apoptosis Data: Variable by site; not uniformly reported
Surface Markers: All sites: CD73+, CD90+, CD105+ (>85%); BUT significant quantitative variation in CD marker expression intensity and HLA-DR baseline (p<0.05). One site reported aberrant CD19 expression.
Functional Assays: T-cell suppression: significant variation among sites (range ~30–75% suppression from same source material; p<0.001). CFU-F counts: significant inter-site variation (p<0.01). Metabolomic profiles: significantly different between sites despite identical input.
Immunomodulation: IDO activity: significant inter-laboratory variation in baseline and IFNγ-induced IDO (p<0.01). Immunosuppressive potency of same-donor MSCs varied >2-fold between best and worst manufacturing sites.
Gene Expression: Gene expression profiles clustered by manufacturing laboratory, not by donor; confirming that manufacturing process dominates MSC transcriptome over donor biology.
Key Quantitative Data Points: T-cell suppression range: ~30–75% across 7 laboratories (same donor source). Expansion fold-change: 10- to 100-fold variation between sites. IDO activity variation: >2-fold inter-laboratory (p<0.01). CD marker intensity CV >30% between sites.
Statistical Tests: ANOVA; inter-group comparisons; Tukey post-hoc; p<0.05 threshold
Key Finding: MSC characteristics vary substantially between manufacturing laboratories even when produced from identical source material. Manufacturing process (medium, passage, freeze protocol) dominates MSC phenotype, transcriptome, and immunopotency over donor biology. Highlights critical need for standardised potency assays and GMP manufacturing SOPs in ATMP supply chains.
Limitations: Single BM donor (no multi-donor validation); site-specific SOPs not published; no in vivo endpoints; cross-sectional (one manufacturing run per site)
Clinical Relevance: Critical – directly demonstrates that manufacturing process variation is the dominant determinant of MSC potency; foundational evidence for functional potency release criteria and GMP SOP standardisation in ATMP regulatory frameworks
